---
name: frontloom-react
description: Use this skill for React and Next.js frontend tasks. Enforces idiomatic React patterns including hooks, component composition, Server vs Client Components, strict typing for props and state, and Next.js App Router conventions. Always use alongside frontloom-core-fe for baseline quality standards.
---

# Frontloom React

Apply this skill to all React and Next.js work.
Use alongside `frontloom-core-fe` for baseline frontend quality standards.

## Goal

Produce React code that is idiomatic, type-safe, composable, and production-ready.
Follow React's mental model — not patterns borrowed from other frameworks.

## Principles

1. Components are functions that return UI based on props and state.
2. Hooks are the composition primitive — use them to share logic, not inheritance or HOCs.
3. Data flows down. Events flow up. Side effects are explicit.
4. Colocation over separation — keep related code together.
5. Composition over configuration — use children and slots, not boolean props.
6. React Server Components are the default in Next.js App Router. Add `"use client"` only when needed.
7. Embrace concurrent features — `useTransition` and `useDeferredValue` exist for a reason.
8. Error boundaries are architectural — plan where failures are caught, not just how they render.

---

## Component Patterns

### Function Components Only

Always use function components. Never use class components in new code.

```tsx
// Correct
function UserCard({ user }: UserCardProps) {
  return (
    <article className="user-card">
      <h3>{user.name}</h3>
      <p>{user.email}</p>
    </article>
  );
}

// Avoid
class UserCard extends React.Component<UserCardProps> {
  render() {
    return <div>{this.props.user.name}</div>;
  }
}
```

### Props Typing

Define props with an interface or type alias. Never use inline object types for non-trivial components.

```tsx
interface UserCardProps {
  user: User;
  onSelect?: (userId: string) => void;
  variant?: "compact" | "full";
}

function UserCard({ user, onSelect, variant = "full" }: UserCardProps) {
  // ...
}
```

### Children Pattern

Use `React.ReactNode` for children. Use render props or slots only when the consumer needs to control rendering.

```tsx
interface CardProps {
  children: React.ReactNode;
  header?: React.ReactNode;
}

function Card({ children, header }: CardProps) {
  return (
    <section className="card">
      {header && <header className="card-header">{header}</header>}
      <div className="card-body">{children}</div>
    </section>
  );
}
```

### Forwarding Refs

Use `ref` as a prop directly (React 19+). For older versions, use `forwardRef`.

```tsx
// React 19+
interface InputProps extends React.ComponentProps<"input"> {
  label: string;
}

function Input({ label, ref, ...props }: InputProps) {
  const id = React.useId();
  return (
    <div>
      <label htmlFor={id}>{label}</label>
      <input ref={ref} id={id} {...props} />
    </div>
  );
}

// React 18 and below
const Input = React.forwardRef<HTMLInputElement, InputProps>(
  function Input({ label, ...props }, ref) {
    const id = React.useId();
    return (
      <div>
        <label htmlFor={id}>{label}</label>
        <input ref={ref} id={id} {...props} />
      </div>
    );
  }
);
```

### Compound Components

Use compound components for related UI that shares implicit state.

```tsx
interface TabsContextValue {
  activeTab: string;
  setActiveTab: (id: string) => void;
}

const TabsContext = React.createContext<TabsContextValue | null>(null);

function useTabsContext() {
  const context = React.useContext(TabsContext);
  if (!context) {
    throw new Error("Tabs compound components must be used within <Tabs>");
  }
  return context;
}

function Tabs({ defaultTab, children }: TabsProps) {
  const [activeTab, setActiveTab] = React.useState(defaultTab);
  return (
    <TabsContext value={{ activeTab, setActiveTab }}>
      <div role="tablist">{children}</div>
    </TabsContext>
  );
}

function TabTrigger({ id, children }: TabTriggerProps) {
  const { activeTab, setActiveTab } = useTabsContext();
  return (
    <button
      role="tab"
      aria-selected={activeTab === id}
      onClick={() => setActiveTab(id)}
    >
      {children}
    </button>
  );
}

function TabContent({ id, children }: TabContentProps) {
  const { activeTab } = useTabsContext();
  if (activeTab !== id) return null;
  return <div role="tabpanel">{children}</div>;
}

Tabs.Trigger = TabTrigger;
Tabs.Content = TabContent;
```

### Composition Over Configuration

Prefer composable components over prop-heavy ones.

```tsx
// Avoid — configuration explosion
<Card
  title="Settings"
  subtitle="Manage your account"
  icon={<GearIcon />}
  footer={<SaveButton />}
  headerAction={<CloseButton />}
  bordered
  padded
/>

// Prefer — composable structure
<Card>
  <Card.Header>
    <Card.Title>Settings</Card.Title>
    <Card.Description>Manage your account</Card.Description>
    <CloseButton />
  </Card.Header>
  <Card.Body>
    {/* content */}
  </Card.Body>
  <Card.Footer>
    <SaveButton />
  </Card.Footer>
</Card>
```

Use composition when:
- The component has more than 3 optional layout slots.
- The consumer needs to control rendering order.
- Different use cases need different internal structures.

Use props when:
- The interface is simple and predictable (variant, size, disabled).
- The internal structure is always the same.

---

## Error Boundaries

Error boundaries are an architectural decision — they define where failures are isolated.

### Basic Error Boundary

```tsx
"use client";

import { Component, type ErrorInfo, type ReactNode } from "react";

interface ErrorBoundaryProps {
  children: ReactNode;
  fallback: ReactNode | ((error: Error, reset: () => void) => ReactNode);
  onError?: (error: Error, errorInfo: ErrorInfo) => void;
}

interface ErrorBoundaryState {
  error: Error | null;
}

class ErrorBoundary extends Component<ErrorBoundaryProps, ErrorBoundaryState> {
  state: ErrorBoundaryState = { error: null };

  static getDerivedStateFromError(error: Error): ErrorBoundaryState {
    return { error };
  }

  componentDidCatch(error: Error, errorInfo: ErrorInfo) {
    this.props.onError?.(error, errorInfo);
  }

  reset = () => {
    this.setState({ error: null });
  };

  render() {
    if (this.state.error) {
      const { fallback } = this.props;
      if (typeof fallback === "function") {
        return fallback(this.state.error, this.reset);
      }
      return fallback;
    }
    return this.props.children;
  }
}
```

### Placement Strategy

```tsx
// Page-level — catch fatal errors, show full-page fallback
<ErrorBoundary fallback={<FullPageError />}>
  <DashboardPage />
</ErrorBoundary>

// Section-level — isolate failures so the rest of the page works
<main>
  <DashboardHeader />
  <ErrorBoundary fallback={(error, reset) => (
    <ErrorCard error={error.message} onRetry={reset} />
  )}>
    <DashboardMetrics />
  </ErrorBoundary>
  <ErrorBoundary fallback={<p>Activity unavailable</p>}>
    <RecentActivity />
  </ErrorBoundary>
</main>

// Widget-level — for third-party or unstable components
<ErrorBoundary fallback={null}>
  <ExperimentalFeature />
</ErrorBoundary>
```

Rules:
- Place at least one error boundary per route/page.
- Use granular boundaries around independently failing sections.
- Log errors via `onError` to your monitoring service.
- Never catch errors silently — always log or surface them.

---

## Hooks

### Rules

1. Only call hooks at the top level of a function component or a custom hook.
2. Name custom hooks with `use` prefix.
3. A custom hook is justified when it encapsulates logic used in 2+ components, or when it simplifies a complex component.

### State

Use `useState` for simple, independent values. Use `useReducer` when state transitions are complex or values depend on each other.

```tsx
// Simple independent state
const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = React.useState(false);

// Complex interdependent state
type FormState = {
  values: Record<string, string>;
  errors: Record<string, string>;
  status: "idle" | "submitting" | "submitted" | "error";
};

type FormAction =
  | { type: "SET_FIELD"; field: string; value: string }
  | { type: "SET_ERRORS"; errors: Record<string, string> }
  | { type: "SUBMIT" }
  | { type: "SUBMIT_SUCCESS" }
  | { type: "SUBMIT_ERROR"; error: string };

function formReducer(state: FormState, action: FormAction): FormState {
  switch (action.type) {
    case "SET_FIELD":
      return {
        ...state,
        values: { ...state.values, [action.field]: action.value },
        errors: { ...state.errors, [action.field]: "" },
      };
    case "SET_ERRORS":
      return { ...state, errors: action.errors, status: "idle" };
    case "SUBMIT":
      return { ...state, status: "submitting" };
    case "SUBMIT_SUCCESS":
      return { ...state, status: "submitted" };
    case "SUBMIT_ERROR":
      return { ...state, status: "error" };
  }
}
```

### Effects

Use `useEffect` only for synchronization with external systems. Never use it for derived state or event handling.

```tsx
// Correct — syncing with external system
useEffect(() => {
  const controller = new AbortController();
  const ws = new WebSocket(url);
  ws.onmessage = (event) => setMessages((prev) => [...prev, event.data]);
  return () => ws.close();
}, [url]);

// Avoid — derived state belongs in render
useEffect(() => {
  setFilteredItems(items.filter((item) => item.active));
}, [items]);

// Correct — derive during render
const filteredItems = items.filter((item) => item.active);

// Correct — expensive derivation
const filteredItems = React.useMemo(
  () => items.filter((item) => matchesComplexCriteria(item, filters)),
  [items, filters]
);
```

### Custom Hook Example

```tsx
function useMediaQuery(query: string): boolean {
  const [matches, setMatches] = React.useState(() =>
    window.matchMedia(query).matches
  );

  useEffect(() => {
    const mediaQuery = window.matchMedia(query);
    const handler = (event: MediaQueryListEvent) => setMatches(event.matches);
    mediaQuery.addEventListener("change", handler);
    return () => mediaQuery.removeEventListener("change", handler);
  }, [query]);

  return matches;
}
```

---

## Concurrent Features

### useTransition

Use `useTransition` to mark state updates as non-urgent, keeping the UI responsive during expensive operations.

```tsx
function FilterableList({ items }: { items: Item[] }) {
  const [query, setQuery] = React.useState("");
  const [isPending, startTransition] = React.useTransition();
  const [filteredItems, setFilteredItems] = React.useState(items);

  function handleSearch(value: string) {
    setQuery(value); // Urgent — update the input immediately
    startTransition(() => {
      setFilteredItems(items.filter((item) => matchesQuery(item, value))); // Non-urgent
    });
  }

  return (
    <div>
      <input
        value={query}
        onChange={(e) => handleSearch(e.target.value)}
        aria-label="Search items"
      />
      {isPending && <p aria-live="polite" className="sr-only">Filtering results…</p>}
      <ItemList items={filteredItems} />
    </div>
  );
}
```

### useDeferredValue

Use `useDeferredValue` when you receive a value from a parent and want to defer re-rendering a subtree.

```tsx
function SearchResults({ query }: { query: string }) {
  const deferredQuery = React.useDeferredValue(query);
  const isStale = query !== deferredQuery;

  return (
    <div style={{ opacity: isStale ? 0.7 : 1 }} aria-busy={isStale}>
      <Results query={deferredQuery} />
    </div>
  );
}
```

### useOptimistic (React 19)

Use `useOptimistic` for optimistic UI updates tied to async actions.

```tsx
"use client";

import { useOptimistic } from "react";

function MessageList({
  messages,
  sendMessage,
}: {
  messages: Message[];
  sendMessage: (text: string) => Promise<void>;
}) {
  const [optimisticMessages, addOptimisticMessage] = useOptimistic(
    messages,
    (current, newMessage: string) => [
      ...current,
      { id: crypto.randomUUID(), text: newMessage, status: "sending" as const },
    ]
  );

  async function handleSend(formData: FormData) {
    const text = formData.get("message") as string;
    addOptimisticMessage(text);
    await sendMessage(text);
  }

  return (
    <>
      <ul role="list">
        {optimisticMessages.map((msg) => (
          <li key={msg.id} aria-busy={msg.status === "sending"}>
            {msg.text}
            {msg.status === "sending" && <span className="sending-indicator">Sending…</span>}
          </li>
        ))}
      </ul>
      <form action={handleSend}>
        <label htmlFor="message">Message</label>
        <input id="message" name="message" required />
        <button type="submit">Send</button>
      </form>
    </>
  );
}
```

### useFormStatus (React 19)

Use `useFormStatus` inside form children to react to the parent form's submission state.

```tsx
"use client";

import { useFormStatus } from "react-dom";

function SubmitButton({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const { pending } = useFormStatus();
  return (
    <button type="submit" disabled={pending} aria-busy={pending}>
      {pending ? "Submitting…" : children}
    </button>
  );
}

// Usage — must be inside a <form>
<form action={serverAction}>
  <input name="email" type="email" required />
  <SubmitButton>Subscribe</SubmitButton>
</form>
```

### Decision Guide

| Scenario | Use |
|---|---|
| Input that triggers expensive filtering/sorting | `useTransition` |
| Parent passes a value that causes heavy re-renders | `useDeferredValue` |
| Optimistic mutation tied to a server action | `useOptimistic` |
| Show pending state inside a form being submitted | `useFormStatus` |
| Navigation between routes | `useTransition` (via `router.push` inside transition) |

---

## Data Fetching

### Server Components (Next.js App Router)

Fetch data in Server Components by default. This keeps the client bundle small and avoids loading states for initial data.

```tsx
// app/users/page.tsx — Server Component (default)
async function UsersPage() {
  const users = await getUsers();

  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Users</h1>
      <UserList users={users} />
    </main>
  );
}
```

### Parallel Data Fetching

Fetch independent data in parallel — never waterfall sequential awaits.

```tsx
// Wrong — sequential waterfall
async function DashboardPage() {
  const user = await getUser();       // 200ms
  const metrics = await getMetrics(); // 300ms
  const activity = await getActivity(); // 250ms
  // Total: 750ms

  return <Dashboard user={user} metrics={metrics} activity={activity} />;
}

// Correct — parallel fetching
async function DashboardPage() {
  const [user, metrics, activity] = await Promise.all([
    getUser(),      // 200ms
    getMetrics(),   // 300ms
    getActivity(),  // 250ms
  ]);
  // Total: 300ms (longest request)

  return <Dashboard user={user} metrics={metrics} activity={activity} />;
}

// Even better — stream non-critical data with Suspense
async function DashboardPage() {
  const user = await getUser(); // Critical — await

  return (
    <main>
      <DashboardHeader user={user} />
      <Suspense fallback={<MetricsSkeleton />}>
        <DashboardMetrics />  {/* Fetches and streams independently */}
      </Suspense>
      <Suspense fallback={<ActivitySkeleton />}>
        <RecentActivity />    {/* Fetches and streams independently */}
      </Suspense>
    </main>
  );
}
```

### Request Deduplication with `cache()`

Use `React.cache()` to deduplicate data fetches across Server Components in the same request.

```tsx
import { cache } from "react";

// This function can be called from multiple Server Components
// but only executes once per request
export const getUser = cache(async (userId: string) => {
  const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${userId}`);
  return res.json() as Promise<User>;
});

// Both components call getUser("123") — only one fetch happens
async function UserHeader({ userId }: { userId: string }) {
  const user = await getUser(userId);
  return <h1>{user.name}</h1>;
}

async function UserSidebar({ userId }: { userId: string }) {
  const user = await getUser(userId);
  return <aside>{user.bio}</aside>;
}
```

### Client-Side Fetching

When data must be fetched on the client (user-driven queries, real-time data), use a data fetching library that the project already uses (TanStack Query, SWR, etc).

```tsx
"use client";

import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

function SearchResults({ query }: { query: string }) {
  const { data, error, isLoading } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["search", query],
    queryFn: () => searchItems(query),
    enabled: query.length > 0,
  });

  if (isLoading) return <SearchSkeleton />;
  if (error) return <ErrorMessage error={error} />;
  if (!data?.length) return <EmptyState message="No results found" />;

  return (
    <ul role="list">
      {data.map((item) => (
        <li key={item.id}>
          <SearchResultCard item={item} />
        </li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}
```

### Server Actions (Next.js)

Use Server Actions for mutations. Pair with `useActionState` for form state management.

```tsx
// actions.ts
"use server";

import { z } from "zod";

const CreateUserSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1, "Name is required"),
  email: z.string().email("Invalid email"),
});

type CreateUserState = {
  errors?: Record<string, string[]>;
  message?: string;
};

export async function createUser(
  prevState: CreateUserState,
  formData: FormData
): Promise<CreateUserState> {
  const parsed = CreateUserSchema.safeParse({
    name: formData.get("name"),
    email: formData.get("email"),
  });

  if (!parsed.success) {
    return { errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors };
  }

  await db.user.create({ data: parsed.data });
  revalidatePath("/users");
  return { message: "User created" };
}
```

```tsx
// create-user-form.tsx
"use client";

import { useActionState } from "react";
import { createUser } from "./actions";

function CreateUserForm() {
  const [state, action, isPending] = useActionState(createUser, {});

  return (
    <form action={action}>
      <div>
        <label htmlFor="name">Name</label>
        <input id="name" name="name" required />
        {state.errors?.name && (
          <p role="alert" className="error">
            {state.errors.name[0]}
          </p>
        )}
      </div>

      <div>
        <label htmlFor="email">Email</label>
        <input id="email" name="email" type="email" required />
        {state.errors?.email && (
          <p role="alert" className="error">
            {state.errors.email[0]}
          </p>
        )}
      </div>

      <button type="submit" disabled={isPending}>
        {isPending ? "Creating…" : "Create User"}
      </button>

      {state.message && (
        <p role="status" className="success">
          {state.message}
        </p>
      )}
    </form>
  );
}
```

---

## Next.js App Router

### Server vs Client Components

**Server Components (default):**
- Fetch data
- Access backend resources
- Keep secrets on the server
- Reduce client bundle

**Client Components (`"use client"`):**
- Use hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`, etc.)
- Use browser APIs
- Add event listeners
- Manage interactive state

```
// Decision guide:
// Does it need interactivity or browser APIs? → "use client"
// Does it only render data? → Server Component
// Does it use hooks? → "use client"
// Does it fetch data that doesn't change per interaction? → Server Component
```

### Layout and Loading Patterns

```tsx
// app/dashboard/layout.tsx
export default function DashboardLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <div className="dashboard-layout">
      <nav aria-label="Dashboard navigation">
        <DashboardNav />
      </nav>
      <main>{children}</main>
    </div>
  );
}

// app/dashboard/loading.tsx
export default function DashboardLoading() {
  return <DashboardSkeleton />;
}

// app/dashboard/error.tsx
"use client";

export default function DashboardError({
  error,
  reset,
}: {
  error: Error & { digest?: string };
  reset: () => void;
}) {
  return (
    <div role="alert">
      <h2>Something went wrong</h2>
      <p>{error.message}</p>
      <button onClick={reset}>Try again</button>
    </div>
  );
}
```

### Streaming with Suspense

```tsx
import { Suspense } from "react";

async function DashboardPage() {
  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Dashboard</h1>

      {/* Critical data loads with the page */}
      <DashboardHeader />

      {/* Non-critical data streams in */}
      <Suspense fallback={<MetricsSkeleton />}>
        <DashboardMetrics />
      </Suspense>

      <Suspense fallback={<ActivitySkeleton />}>
        <RecentActivity />
      </Suspense>
    </main>
  );
}
```

### Metadata and SEO (Next.js)

Always export metadata from page and layout Server Components.

```tsx
// app/users/page.tsx
import type { Metadata } from "next";

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: "Users — Dashboard",
  description: "Manage your team members and their permissions.",
};

// Dynamic metadata
export async function generateMetadata({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}): Promise<Metadata> {
  const { id } = await params;
  const user = await getUser(id);
  return {
    title: `${user.name} — Profile`,
    description: `View ${user.name}'s profile and activity.`,
    openGraph: {
      title: user.name,
      images: [{ url: user.avatarUrl }],
    },
  };
}
```

Rules:
- Every public page must have a `title` and `description`.
- Use `generateMetadata` when metadata depends on dynamic data.
- Set `openGraph` and `twitter` metadata for shareable pages.
- Use the `robots` field to control indexing per page when needed.

### Route Handlers (API Routes)

Use route handlers for webhooks, third-party integrations, and cases where you need a raw HTTP endpoint.

```tsx
// app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts
import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from "next/server";

export async function POST(request: NextRequest) {
  const body = await request.text();
  const signature = request.headers.get("stripe-signature");

  if (!signature) {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "Missing signature" }, { status: 400 });
  }

  try {
    const event = verifyStripeWebhook(body, signature);
    await handleStripeEvent(event);
    return NextResponse.json({ received: true });
  } catch {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "Invalid signature" }, { status: 401 });
  }
}
```

Rules:
- Prefer Server Actions over Route Handlers for form mutations.
- Use Route Handlers for external webhooks, file downloads, and streaming responses.
- Always validate input and authenticate requests.

### Middleware

Use middleware for cross-cutting concerns that run before every (or some) requests.

```tsx
// middleware.ts (at project root)
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  // Auth check
  const token = request.cookies.get("session")?.value;

  if (!token && request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith("/dashboard")) {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL("/login", request.url));
  }

  // Add headers
  const response = NextResponse.next();
  response.headers.set("x-pathname", request.nextUrl.pathname);
  return response;
}

export const config = {
  matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*", "/settings/:path*"],
};
```

Middleware is right for: auth redirects, geolocation, A/B testing headers, request logging.
Middleware is wrong for: data fetching, heavy computation, database queries (it runs on the Edge).

---

## State Management

### Decision Guide

| Scope | Solution |
|---|---|
| Local to one component | `useState` or `useReducer` |
| Shared between parent-child | Props |
| Shared across a subtree | Context |
| Global client state | Zustand, Jotai, or project's existing store |
| Server state | TanStack Query, SWR, or Server Components |
| URL state | `useSearchParams`, `nuqs` |

### Context Pattern

Use context for dependency injection, not global state management.

```tsx
interface ThemeContextValue {
  theme: "light" | "dark";
  toggleTheme: () => void;
}

const ThemeContext = React.createContext<ThemeContextValue | null>(null);

export function useTheme() {
  const context = React.useContext(ThemeContext);
  if (!context) {
    throw new Error("useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider");
  }
  return context;
}

export function ThemeProvider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const [theme, setTheme] = React.useState<"light" | "dark">("light");
  const toggleTheme = React.useCallback(() => {
    setTheme((prev) => (prev === "light" ? "dark" : "light"));
  }, []);

  return (
    <ThemeContext value={{ theme, toggleTheme }}>
      {children}
    </ThemeContext>
  );
}
```

### URL State

Treat URL as a state manager for anything that should survive refresh, back/forward, and sharing.

```tsx
"use client";

import { useSearchParams, useRouter, usePathname } from "next/navigation";

function useQueryState(key: string, defaultValue: string = "") {
  const searchParams = useSearchParams();
  const router = useRouter();
  const pathname = usePathname();

  const value = searchParams.get(key) ?? defaultValue;

  function setValue(newValue: string) {
    const params = new URLSearchParams(searchParams.toString());
    if (newValue === defaultValue) {
      params.delete(key);
    } else {
      params.set(key, newValue);
    }
    router.replace(`${pathname}?${params.toString()}`);
  }

  return [value, setValue] as const;
}

// Usage
function ProductsPage() {
  const [sort, setSort] = useQueryState("sort", "newest");
  const [category, setCategory] = useQueryState("category");
  // URL: /products?sort=price&category=electronics
}
```

URL state is right for: filters, sort order, pagination, search queries, selected tab, open modal by ID.
React state is right for: hover, focus, animation progress, form field values before submission.

---

## Performance

### When to Optimize

Do not optimize prematurely. Optimize when:
- You can measure a real performance problem.
- The component re-renders frequently with expensive computations.
- A parent re-renders and passes new object/array references to children.

### Memoization

```tsx
// Expensive computation
const sortedItems = React.useMemo(
  () => items.toSorted((a, b) => a.score - b.score),
  [items]
);

// Stable callback for child components
const handleSelect = React.useCallback(
  (id: string) => {
    dispatch({ type: "SELECT", id });
  },
  [dispatch]
);

// Prevent re-renders of expensive child
const MemoizedChart = React.memo(function Chart({ data }: ChartProps) {
  // expensive rendering
  return <canvas />;
});
```

### Code Splitting

```tsx
// Lazy load heavy components
const AdminPanel = React.lazy(() => import("./AdminPanel"));

function App() {
  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<LoadingSpinner />}>
      {isAdmin && <AdminPanel />}
    </Suspense>
  );
}
```

### Bundle Awareness

Think about what you're sending to the client.

```tsx
// Wrong — imports the entire library into the client bundle
"use client";
import { format } from "date-fns";

// Better — import only what you need
"use client";
import { format } from "date-fns/format";

// Best — format on the server, send the string
// Server Component (no "use client")
import { format } from "date-fns";
function EventDate({ date }: { date: Date }) {
  return <time dateTime={date.toISOString()}>{format(date, "PPP")}</time>;
}
```

Rules:
- Check bundle impact before adding new dependencies — use `bundlephobia.com` or `packagephobia.com`.
- Prefer tree-shakeable imports.
- Move formatting, computation, and heavy libraries to Server Components when possible.
- Audit `"use client"` boundaries — each one defines what ships to the browser.
```

---

## Testing Patterns

Follow the project's existing testing setup. These patterns apply generally.

### Component Testing

Test behavior, not implementation.

```tsx
import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import userEvent from "@testing-library/user-event";

test("submits the form with user input", async () => {
  const user = userEvent.setup();
  const onSubmit = vi.fn();

  render(<ContactForm onSubmit={onSubmit} />);

  await user.type(screen.getByLabelText("Name"), "Jane");
  await user.type(screen.getByLabelText("Email"), "jane@example.com");
  await user.click(screen.getByRole("button", { name: "Submit" }));

  expect(onSubmit).toHaveBeenCalledWith({
    name: "Jane",
    email: "jane@example.com",
  });
});
```

### Query Priority

Follow Testing Library's query priority:
1. `getByRole` — accessible queries
2. `getByLabelText` — form fields
3. `getByPlaceholderText` — fallback for unlabeled inputs
4. `getByText` — non-interactive elements
5. `getByTestId` — last resort

### Async Testing

```tsx
test("loads and displays user data", async () => {
  render(<UserProfile userId="123" />);

  // Wait for loading to finish
  expect(screen.getByRole("status")).toHaveTextContent("Loading");

  // Wait for content
  const heading = await screen.findByRole("heading", { name: "Jane Doe" });
  expect(heading).toBeInTheDocument();
});
```

---

## Common Anti-Patterns to Avoid

### Do Not

- **Use `useEffect` for derived state.** Compute it during render or with `useMemo`.
- **Create state for values derivable from props.** Derive inline.
- **Prop-drill through 5+ levels.** Use context or composition.
- **Use `index` as key for dynamic lists.** Use a stable unique identifier.
- **Put everything in `"use client"`.** Default to Server Components. Add `"use client"` only when the component needs interactivity.
- **Fetch in `useEffect` without cleanup.** Use a data fetching library or handle abort controllers.
- **Create god components.** Split when a component handles multiple responsibilities.
- **Use `any` to silence TypeScript.** Fix the type instead.
- **Wrap every component in `React.memo`.** Memo is not free — use it when profiling shows it helps.
- **Store server state in `useState`.** Use Server Components or a query library.
- **Use `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` without sanitization.** Always sanitize with DOMPurify or similar.
- **Put shareable state in React state.** Filters, pagination, and sort order belong in the URL.
- **Ignore error boundaries.** Every route should have at least one.
- **Block the main thread with expensive renders.** Use `useTransition` or `useDeferredValue`.
- **Add dependencies without checking bundle size.** Check with bundlephobia or similar before importing.

---

## When To Read References

Read only the files that match the current task:

- `references/semantic-html.md` — creating or restructuring markup
- `references/accessibility.md` — forms, dialogs, navigation, interactive UI
- `references/typescript-strict.md` — props, API data, state typing
- `references/ui-states.md` — loading, empty, error, success states
- `references/security.md` — user input, external content, links
- `references/responsive.md` — layouts, viewport-dependent UI
- `references/testing.md` — testing React components, hooks, async flows, MSW
- `references/performance.md` — Core Web Vitals, Next.js rendering strategy, bundle optimization
- `references/css-architecture.md` — styling decisions, theming, design tokens
- `references/review-checklist.md` — final review gate

## Boundaries

This skill provides React and Next.js idioms.
For cross-framework standards (semantics, accessibility, typing, UX states), defer to `frontloom-core-fe`.
